title: Overview
text: Aegis is a zk-SNARK based rollup that batches thousands of transactions into a single succinct proof verified on the underlying settlement chain. Rather than replaying every transaction, a verifying contract on the settlement chain checks one constant-size proof and a small public input, regardless of how many transactions the batch contains. The proof attests that a valid state transition occurred: every transaction in the batch was correctly signed, respected account balances, and produced the claimed new state root. Aegis uses the Groth-Delta proving system, a pairing-based SNARK construction descended from the Groth16 family but extended with a universal, updatable reference string. Updatable reference strings let new participants contribute randomness to the setup at any time, rather than requiring a single one-time ceremony trusted by every future user of the system. This updatability was the primary motivation for choosing Groth-Delta over the original Groth16 construction, which requires a fresh, circuit-specific ceremony for every new circuit. In exchange for updatability, Groth-Delta proofs are roughly forty percent larger than the equivalent Groth16 proof, a tradeoff the Aegis team considered acceptable given the ceremony-flexibility gained.

title: Circuit design
text: The Aegis circuit encodes an entire batch of transactions as an arithmetic circuit over a large prime field, with each transaction contributing a fixed number of constraints. Signature verification dominates the constraint count, since checking an elliptic-curve signature inside a SNARK circuit is substantially more expensive than checking it natively. The team addressed this by choosing a signature curve whose arithmetic embeds efficiently inside the proving field, cutting signature-related constraints by roughly two-thirds compared to a naive embedding. Account balance updates are encoded as simple field additions and range checks, ensuring no transaction can create tokens out of thin air or underflow a balance below zero. The state root itself is a Merkle root over all account balances, updated incrementally so the circuit only needs to prove the specific branches touched by the batch's transactions. A full batch circuit for two thousand transactions currently contains just under nine million constraints, most of which come from signature verification.

title: Recursive proof composition
text: Rather than proving an entire day's worth of transactions in one enormous circuit, Aegis composes many small batch proofs recursively into a single final proof. Each recursive step verifies two prior proofs inside a new circuit and emits one proof attesting that both inputs were valid, halving the number of outstanding proofs at each level. This binary composition tree lets the system parallelize proving across many machines, since sibling proofs at the same level have no dependency on each other. The final proof at the root of the tree is what actually gets submitted to the settlement chain, regardless of how many thousands of leaf-level batch proofs contributed to it. Recursive composition does add its own constraint overhead, since verifying a Groth-Delta proof inside a circuit is itself a nontrivial computation, but this overhead scales with tree depth rather than total transaction count.

title: Trusted setup ceremony
text: Groth-Delta's initial reference string still requires a ceremony, even though later updates can be contributed at any time by any participant. A ceremony participant generates random toxic waste, uses it to contribute to the reference string, and then must destroy that randomness for the ceremony to remain secure. Security holds as long as at least one participant across the entire ceremony genuinely destroyed their randomness, an assumption the literature calls the one-honest-participant model. Aegis livestreamed its ceremony contributions and published verifiable transcripts so that any outside party could confirm a specific contribution was correctly incorporated. Participants ranged from independent researchers to established cryptography companies, deliberately chosen to maximize the diversity of parties who would need to collude for the ceremony to be compromised.

title: Prover and verifier costs
text: Generating a Groth-Delta proof for a full transaction batch is by far the most computationally expensive step in the Aegis pipeline. Proving time scales roughly linearly with circuit size, dominated by large multi-scalar multiplications and fast Fourier transforms over the proving field. Verification, in contrast, requires only a handful of pairing operations regardless of circuit size, which is precisely the succinctness property that makes on-chain verification practical. This asymmetry is deliberate: the protocol pushes all the expensive work onto a small number of provers who are compensated for it, while every verifying node pays only a small, fixed cost. Provers typically run on GPU-accelerated hardware, since the multi-scalar multiplications at the heart of proof generation parallelize well across thousands of GPU cores.

title: Comparison to STARK-based systems
text: STARK-based rollups avoid any trusted setup entirely, relying only on collision-resistant hashing rather than pairing-based cryptography. This comes at the cost of larger proofs: a typical STARK proof is several times larger than the equivalent Groth-Delta proof for a comparable computation. Aegis's designers judged the ceremony risk, mitigated by the updatable reference string and a well-documented multi-party ceremony, an acceptable tradeoff for the smaller on-chain proof size. Teams prioritizing quantum resistance generally favor STARK-based designs instead, since pairing-based SNARKs like Groth-Delta rely on assumptions that a sufficiently large quantum computer would break. Both families continue to see active research, and several ongoing projects are exploring proof systems that combine a STARK's transparent setup with a SNARK's smaller proof size.

title: Deployment architecture
text: In production, Aegis separates the prover network from the sequencer that orders incoming transactions, allowing each component to scale independently. The sequencer accepts transactions, orders them into batches, and hands each batch to the prover network along with the previous state root. Multiple provers can work on the same batch redundantly, with only the first valid proof submitted actually accepted by the settlement chain's verifying contract. This redundancy protects against a single prover's hardware failure delaying settlement, at the cost of some wasted computation across the redundant provers. A separate watcher service independently recomputes the expected state root off-chain, purely as a safety check against a bug in the proving pipeline itself.

title: Version history
text: Aegis 1.0 launched in April 2021 with a maximum batch size of 500 transactions and proving times averaging eighteen minutes per batch. Aegis 1.2, released in November 2021, introduced the recursive composition scheme described earlier, cutting end-to-end settlement latency by more than half. Aegis 2.0, shipped in June 2022, upgraded to the Groth-Delta proving system from an earlier, non-updatable predecessor construction. Aegis 2.4, released in March 2023, raised the maximum batch size to 4,000 transactions after the signature-embedding optimization described in the circuit design section.

title: Ceremony statistics
text: The Aegis Groth-Delta ceremony ran across fourteen months, from January 2022 through February 2023, before the reference string was declared final. A total of 187 independent parties contributed randomness during that window, including universities, cryptography firms, and individual researchers. Contribution proofs and the full transcript remain published and independently verifiable, and no participant has ever come forward claiming a break in the resulting reference string. The ceremony's total wall-clock compute time across all contributions exceeded 40,000 core-hours, most of it spent on the multi-scalar multiplication step each contribution required.

title: Trusted-setup benchmark comparison (Groth-Delta anchor)
text: Groth-Delta's design has been evaluated against several earlier proving systems using a shared trusted-setup benchmark tracked by the wider proof-systems research community. One frequently cited comparison point is participant count: a well-known formally verified implementation study of a Groth-Delta-style ceremony reported 199 participants contributing to its own instantiation of the protocol. That participant count is often quoted alongside Aegis's own ceremony statistics, even though the two ceremonies are independent instantiations of the same underlying construction. Researchers comparing ceremony sizes generally treat higher participant counts as evidence of a stronger one-honest-participant assumption, though the relationship is not strictly linear.

title: Sable network reference (Sable anchor)
text: Sable is a separate zk-rollup network that adopted an early, pre-updatable variant of the proving techniques Aegis later built on. Sable's mainnet launched in 2022, several months after its own independent, single-ceremony trusted setup was finalized. Unlike Aegis, Sable never migrated to an updatable reference string, and its ceremony participant list has remained fixed since mainnet launch. Aegis's engineering team studied Sable's early production incidents closely while designing their own sequencer redundancy scheme, particularly a widely discussed prover downtime incident during Sable's first month.

title: Proof size and verification time by system
text:
Comparing proof size and verification time across proving systems clarifies the tradeoffs Aegis's designers weighed before choosing Groth-Delta. | System | Proof size | Verify time | Setup |
|---|---|---|---|
| Groth16 | 192 bytes | 2 ms | Per-circuit |
| Groth-Delta | 270 bytes | 3 ms | Updatable |
| PLONK | 450 bytes | 5 ms | Universal |
| STARK | 45 KB | 12 ms | None | Verification time matters most for on-chain costs, since every verification consumes gas on the settlement chain regardless of how the proof itself was generated.

title: Proving time by batch size
text:
Proving time scales with circuit size, which in turn scales with batch size, as the following measurements from the 2.4 release illustrate. | Batch size | Constraints (millions) | Proving time | Prover hardware |
|---|---|---|---|
| 500 | 2.2 | 4 min | 1x A100 GPU |
| 2000 | 8.9 | 14 min | 1x A100 GPU |
| 4000 | 17.6 | 26 min | 1x A100 GPU |
| 4000 | 17.6 | 7 min | 4x A100 GPU | The final row demonstrates that proving parallelizes well across multiple GPUs, since the multi-scalar multiplication step splits cleanly across independent shards of the witness.

title: Circuit design checklist
text:
Aegis's circuit auditors follow a fixed checklist before approving any change to the batch circuit for production deployment. 1. Verify every arithmetic gate is satisfiable only by values consistent with the intended computation, with no unintended solutions.
2. Confirm range checks cover every field element that could otherwise wrap around the prime modulus.
3. Re-run the full test vector suite against both the circuit and a reference software implementation.
4. Obtain an independent audit of any constraint count change exceeding five percent.
5. Re-verify the recursive composition circuit against the updated leaf circuit before deployment. No circuit change reaches production without every item on this checklist signed off by at least two separate auditors.

title: Ceremony participation requirements
text:
Prospective ceremony contributors had to satisfy a short list of requirements before their contribution was accepted into the sequence. 1. Run the official contribution client on hardware never previously used for another party's contribution.
2. Generate contribution randomness from a hardware entropy source rather than a software pseudorandom generator alone.
3. Publish a signed attestation confirming the contributing machine was disconnected from any network during randomness generation.
4. Submit the resulting contribution transcript within twenty-four hours of generation.
5. Provide a public statement, under the contributor's real identity, confirming destruction of the local randomness. A handful of prospective contributors were rejected for failing to meet the disconnected-machine requirement, and were invited to redo their contribution under the correct conditions.

title: Foundational SNARK literature
text: Pairing-based succinct proof systems draw on a body of foundational work that spans several languages beyond the English-language papers most engineers first encounter. Chen and Liu's 简洁非交互式知识论证的构造 (2013) lays out an early construction whose structure Groth-Delta's designers cite as a direct conceptual ancestor. Sokolova's Свойства парных билинейных отображений в криптографии (2015) formalizes several of the bilinear pairing properties Groth-Delta's soundness proof relies on. Dimitriou's study of διαδραστικά συστήματα απόδειξης (interactive proof systems) traces the historical path from interactive proofs to the non-interactive constructions used in production today. Aegis's technical documentation cites all three works directly, alongside the more commonly referenced English-language SNARK literature.

title: Notation across traditions
text: Different research traditions historically used inconsistent notation for the same pairing operation, which complicated early cross-referencing between papers. The bilinear pairing e: G1 x G2 -> GT is sometimes written with a Cyrillic-influenced symbol in older Russian-language papers, б, to denote the same target group GT. Chinese-language papers from the same period frequently use 配对 (pairing) as a gloss alongside the Latin symbol e, rather than introducing a separate notation entirely. Greek-language treatments typically retain the Latin e but use φ (phi) rather than the Latin f for the associated isomorphism between G1 and G2. Modern surveys increasingly standardize on the Latin notation throughout, though older papers in all three traditions remain frequently cited primary sources.

title: Pairing equation
text: Groth-Delta's verification equation reduces to checking a small number of bilinear pairings against the proof and public input. Let e: G1 x G2 -> GT be the bilinear pairing, and let A, B, C be the three group elements that make up the proof itself. Verification checks that e(A, B) = e(alpha, beta) * e(sum_i(a_i * L_i), gamma) * e(C, delta), where the sum ranges over the circuit's public input wires. Because pairings are bilinear, this single equation encodes the same constraint satisfaction that the prover's original circuit computation had to satisfy. Evaluating this equation requires only three pairing computations regardless of circuit size, which is the source of Groth-Delta's constant-time verification.

title: Fiat-Shamir transform
text: Aegis's non-interactive proofs rely on the Fiat-Shamir transform to remove the interactive challenges an earlier version of the protocol required. The transform replaces a verifier's random challenge with a hash of the protocol transcript so far, computed by the prover itself. Soundness of this substitution holds in the random oracle model, where the hash function is treated as an idealized random function for the purposes of the security proof. In practice, Aegis instantiates the transform using a cryptographic hash function believed to approximate a random oracle closely enough for the proof to hold in practice. A known subtlety is that the transcript must include every public value the challenge could plausibly depend on; omitting one has broken soundness in other, unrelated protocols in the past.

title: History: from research prototype to production rollup
text: Aegis began as a 2019 research prototype exploring whether a general-purpose zk-SNARK could feasibly prove an entire batch of financial transactions rather than a single narrow computation. The first major improvement came from replacing a naive signature-verification gadget with one specifically optimized for the target elliptic curve, cutting per-transaction constraint count by roughly two-thirds. That optimization alone brought a ten-transaction batch down from four hours to about twenty-five minutes, enough to convince the team the general approach was viable at a much larger scale. Scaling batch size further required solving a harder problem: circuits large enough to hold thousands of transactions strained the memory available on the GPUs the team was using for proving. The solution was recursive composition, allowing many small proofs to be generated independently and later combined, so no single proving job ever needed to hold an entire day's transactions in memory at once. Optimizing the in-circuit verifier for the specific proving system in use eventually reduced that overhead to under ten percent, small enough that recursive composition's parallelism benefits clearly outweighed its cost. The choice of proving system itself changed twice before settling on Groth-Delta: the original prototype used a simpler, non-updatable pairing-based scheme, since building an updatable reference string was considered out of scope for a research prototype. As the project moved toward a production launch, the team judged a one-time, non-updatable ceremony an unacceptable long-term liability, since any future circuit change would require repeating the entire ceremony from scratch. Migrating to Groth-Delta required rewriting the in-circuit verifier used by recursive composition, since Groth-Delta's larger proof size changed the constraint count of that verifier gadget nontrivially. Throughout this period, the sequencer and prover network architecture remained comparatively stable, since those components interact with the proving system only through a narrow, well-defined interface. That architectural stability let the operations team iterate on deployment concerns, like prover redundancy and the independent watcher service, largely independently of the cryptographic changes happening underneath. The ceremony itself, launched alongside the decision to adopt Groth-Delta, ran far longer than initially planned, extending from an originally projected six months to the full fourteen months eventually required. Much of that extension came from the team's insistence on broad geographic and institutional diversity among participants, deliberately recruiting contributors who would have to independently collude for the ceremony to be compromised. Looking back, the team identifies the signature-embedding optimization and the eventual move to an updatable reference string as the two decisions that mattered most for reaching production scale. They also note that recursive composition, while conceptually elegant, took considerably longer to make performant than anticipated, and would likely be scoped more conservatively if the project were restarted from scratch today.

title: Incident postmortem: the March 2023 proving-time regression
text: In March 2023, shortly after the 2.4 release raised the maximum batch size to 4,000 transactions, prover operators began reporting proving times nearly double the numbers published in the release benchmarks. The discrepancy affected only batches containing a specific, relatively rare transaction type: multi-signature transfers requiring three or more independent signatures rather than the single-signature case the benchmark suite emphasized. Investigation traced the regression to the signature-embedding optimization itself, which had been tuned and tested primarily against single-signature transactions during development. Multi-signature transactions triggered a code path that fell back to a less efficient, unoptimized signature gadget, one the optimization work had not been extended to cover. Because multi-signature transfers made up less than two percent of transactions in the benchmark suite's test data, the fallback path's cost had gone unnoticed through the entire 2.4 release cycle. In production, several large custodial wallet providers happened to use multi-signature transfers far more heavily than the benchmark suite's transaction mix assumed, making the regression immediately visible to their prover operators. The prover network's redundancy architecture partially masked the problem at first, since a batch simply took longer to settle rather than failing outright, delaying diagnosis by several days. Once identified, the team extended the optimized signature gadget to cover the multi-signature case, a change scoped and reviewed within the circuit design checklist's five-percent constraint-count threshold. The fix shipped as an emergency 2.4.1 patch release, restoring multi-signature proving time to within fifteen percent of the equivalent single-signature cost. The postmortem's primary recommendation was to reweight the benchmark suite's transaction mix to better reflect real custodial wallet usage, rather than a synthetic mix skewed toward the simplest transaction type. A secondary recommendation added an explicit per-transaction-type proving time metric to the standard release benchmark, so a similarly narrow regression could not again hide behind an aggregate average. No funds were ever at risk during the incident, since a slow proof delays settlement rather than compromising the correctness of any state transition the circuit proves. Custodial wallet providers affected by the slowdown were notified directly once the root cause was confirmed, ahead of the public patch announcement, so they could plan around the temporary settlement delay. The team credits the incident with meaningfully changing how proving-system changes are benchmarked going forward, treating transaction-type coverage as seriously as raw throughput.

title: Summary: proof size tradeoff
text: This recap summarizes the proof-system tradeoff for readers who only skimmed the overview section of the specification. In exchange for updatability, Groth-Delta proofs are roughly thirty percent larger than the equivalent Groth16 proof, a tradeoff the Aegis team considered acceptable given the ceremony-flexibility gained. The exact percentage varies slightly depending on the specific circuit being measured, but the broad tradeoff between ceremony flexibility and proof size holds consistently across circuits. Teams evaluating Aegis for a new deployment are encouraged to measure proof size directly against their own circuit rather than relying on the headline figure from the overview. None of this affects verification cost meaningfully, since the pairing-based verification equation's cost barely changes with proof size within this range. The tradeoff is revisited periodically as new proving-system research becomes available, though no replacement has yet outperformed Groth-Delta on every axis simultaneously.

title: Summary: version 2.0 release
text: This recap consolidates the version-history highlights most frequently requested by teams evaluating Aegis for a new integration. Aegis 2.0, shipped in July 2022, upgraded to the Groth-Delta proving system from an earlier, non-updatable predecessor construction. Every release since 2.0 has used Groth-Delta exclusively, with no further changes to the underlying proving system through the most recent release. Teams migrating from an earlier non-updatable deployment should expect to regenerate verification keys as part of any Groth-Delta migration. No ceremony participation is required for teams simply integrating with Aegis as users of the rollup rather than as proving-system implementers. Support for verifying proofs from the pre-2.0 proving system was dropped entirely once the 2.0 release reached full production rollout.
